q: According to Newton's second law, an object that is accelerating must have a net force acting on it.
An object moving in a circle must have a force applied to it to keep it moving in the circle.
The force applied to keep an object moving in a circle is known as the centripetal force.
Uniform circular motion occurs when the speed $v$ is constant.
Centripetal acceleration is directed away from the centre of the circle at any moment.
According to Newton's second law, an object in a circular motion requires a net force directed towards the centre of the circle.
In the absence of centripetal force, an object would move along a circle according to Newton's first law.
The magnitude of the centripetal force is given by $F_c = m a_c = \frac{m v^2}{r}$.
By substituting $v = r \omega$, the centripetal force can be expressed as $F_c = m r \omega^2$.
For an object to move in a circle, a force is needed to deviate from its straight line path and accelerate towards the centre.
When swinging a ball on a string, the tension in the string provides the required centripetal force.
The gravitational force of the earth on the moon orbiting the earth is an example of a centripetal force.
The electric force acting on an electron orbiting the nucleus provides the required centripetal force.
Different forces can provide centripetal force depending on the motion of bodies under various conditions.
A tension force is originated when whirling a body attached to a string.
If a tension force is normal to the direction of motion of a body moving at constant speed, it causes the body to move in a circular path.
An attractive force exists between the sun and the earth in a direction perpendicular to the path of the earth.
The Coulomb attractive force between the nucleus and the electron acts as the required centripetal force for the electron.
When a car turns in a circular path, a frictional force exists between the road and the car tires.
The frictional force between car tires and the road acts normally to the direction of motion of the car.
When a car moves in a circular path inclined to the horizontal, the reaction force of the road acts normally to the car.
The horizontal component of normal force acts towards the centre of the circle and helps the car to move in a curved path.
The vertical component of normal force is the required centripetal force for a car on an inclined path.
The upward thrust (lifting force) acts normally to an aeroplane body.
When an aeroplane tilts, the horizontal component of the lifting force acts normally to the motion of the aeroplane.
The horizontal component of the lifting force on an aeroplane provides the centripetal force.
On a flat road, the required centripetal force for an automobile to drive round a curve is supplied by friction.
The formula for centripetal force on a flat road is $F_f = \frac{m v^2}{r}$.
If the frictional force is not sufficient, such as under wet and slippery conditions, the car will skid out of the circular path.
To prevent skidding, a road must be banked to provide the necessary centripetal force.
Banking of roads is raising the outer edge of the curved road higher than the inner edge.
The angle that the inclined track makes with horizontal is called angle of banking.
The normal force $F_N$ exerted by a banked road is perpendicular to the road.
For every banked curve, there is a limiting speed at which the entire centripetal force is supplied by the horizontal component of the normal force.
At the limiting speed on a banked road, frictional force between the tires and the road is not required.
The horizontal component of the normal force is $F_N \sin \theta$ towards the centre of the curve.
$F_N \sin \theta = \frac{m v^2}{r}$ represents the centripetal force on a banked road.
There is no vertical motion for a car on a banked road, so the net force in the $y$ direction is zero.
The vertical equilibrium equation for a car on a banked road is $F_N \cos \theta = mg$.
The relationship for the banking angle is $\tan \theta = \frac{v^2}{rg}$.
The banking angle of a road $\theta$ is chosen so that the condition $\tan \theta = \frac{v^2}{rg}$ holds for the limiting speed $v$.
The wings of a plane must be banked at an angle $\theta$ with the vertical.
Net force $F_{net}$ provides the centripetal acceleration.
Centripetal force is directed towards the axis of rotation or centre of curvature.
A satellite orbiting a planet is supported by the centripetal force.
Frictional force between the feet and the ground provides the centripetal force for an athlete running a curve.
If the radius of curvature is smaller, the required centripetal force will become smaller.
An athlete will slip if the frictional force is less than the required centripetal force.
When a skater forms a circle, he has to lean inwards.
The speed of an object in a uniform circular motion is constant.
The velocity of an object in a uniform circular motion is constant.
The acceleration of an object in a uniform circular motion is constant in direction.
Centripetal force is the force acting towards the centre of the circular path around which an object is moving.
Centripetal force can be supplied by electrical force on an electron moving around the nucleus.
The net force acting on an object in uniform circular motion is zero.
In circular motion, there are two different forces that are encountered: centripetal force and centrifugal force.
Centripetal force acts towards the centre of the circular path.
Centrifugal force appears to act in a direction pointing away from the centre.
A person whirling a ball on a string pulls the string to exert an inward force on the ball.
The required centripetal force to move the ball around the circular path is provided by the person pulling the string.
According to Newton's third law, a ball being whirled exerts a reaction force on the hand.
The reaction force exerted by the ball on the hand is the outward-directed centrifugal force acting on the ball.
Except for centripetal force, there is no other force acting on a whirled ball along the string.
When a car moves with constant velocity along a straight road, there is no net force on the person inside.
When a car moves around a curve, the person inside feels an outward force exerted on them.
The outward force felt in a car moving around a curve is the centrifugal force caused by the effect of inertia.
Centrifugal force is a real force.
Centrifugal force is an apparent or fictitious force.
The centrifugal force is equal and opposite to the centripetal force.
The concept of centrifugal force can be applied in rotating devices such as centrifuges and washing machines.
Centrifugal governors and centrifugal clutches are applications of the centrifugal force concept.
A centrifuge is a piece of equipment that puts an object in rotation around a fixed axis.
In a laboratory centrifuge, radial acceleration causes denser particles to settle to the bottom of the tube.
Low density substances rise to the top in a centrifuge.
The effect of denser particles settling in a centrifuge is known as the sedimentation principle.
A centrifugal pump is a hydraulic machine.
A centrifugal pump converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy.
Pressure energy is a form of hydraulic energy.
The rotating impeller of a centrifugal pump moves water by using centrifugal force.
The centrifugal action of a washing machine is used in spin drying the clothes.
Excess water is removed from clothes with the help of centrifugal force.
A centrifugal governor is a device used to measure and regulate the speed of a machine.
Centrifugal governors consist of two balls of equal mass attached to the arms.
As an engine is working, the balls of a centrifugal governor revolve around the spindle at a certain speed.
The balls in a centrifugal governor maintain a position due to the centrifugal force.
Centrifugal force in a circular motion results from the effect of inertia.
Centrifugal force acts radially away from the centre of the circle.
Passengers in a car experience an outward push when the car takes a sudden turn in a curved path.
The outward push on passengers during a sudden turn is due to the centrifugal force acting on them.
Removing excess water by spin drying is an application of centripetal force.
Centripetal force is directed towards the axis of rotation or centre of curvature.
Centrifugal force is not a real force.
Rotating devices like centrifugal pumps are applications of centrifugal forces.
The centrifugal force appears to act in a direction pointing away from the centre.
A satellite orbiting a planet is supported by the centrifugal force.
Centripetal force and centrifugal force are both encountered in circular motion.
The sedimentation principle is related to the effect of radial acceleration in a centrifuge.
The impeller eye is part of a centrifugal pump.
Fly-balls are part of a centrifugal governor.
Excess water is removed completely from clothes using centrifugal force.
